segments.text: But I think it was also the singing and setting up of the bonfire,
all of that was just such a treat
because never had she experienced something like that before.
All right, that first year, you didn't encounter any hazing then.
No.
You weren't a fish in the normal sense of the word.
No, I didn't, but I think I began to hear about,
I knew I had some freshmen, and I knew I had friends of mine that were fish.
A lot of my classmates from Bryan did not join the Corps,
though I saw them occasionally.
But I guess those schoolmates of mine that ended up going,
I remember them, and I saw them in their uniform and short hair
and kind of in their state of shock at first.
And I remember them.
I guess the other thing that I remember quite vividly was during the bonfire,
during the cutting of the bonfire, how many of them were just dozing off in class.
And so it was those kinds of experiences that I did remember,
and at least from seeing the students.
All right, describe, you went to class on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.
Describe that day.
You got up at what time, came to campus, did what, and so forth.
I usually would get up probably about, my class was usually about 8 o'clock.
I'd try to get up as early as possible so we could get out by 12.
I imagine we got up about a quarter to 7, got ready,
and of course just lived in West Bryan and just take on Finnfeather.
I could get to school in about 10, 15 minutes and park.
Parking was not a big deal.
Yeah, where did you park?
I parked across from the drill field, that parking lot that still exists there.
And walked the academic building, Nagel Hall, where most of my classes were.
And then where would lunch be?
Lunch would be back home probably because we would eat right before we went to work.
And so my mom would have us some lunch,
and then she'd give us a sack lunch for dinner where we would eat.
But there was my brother, Moises, and I, and another friend named Mario,
and they both would come back home to find lunch.
And they worked to death to get the lunch and take the получить the lunch.
So it was pretty much the same, the three of us pretty much had the same routine.
So we'd see each other get in their cars, leaving or whatever, about the same time.
I did start getting involved with school stuff later,
but when I guess I started being able to work a little less.
about a repeat of the fall in terms of the, you took.
Another 12, 13 hours and, you know,
I was always a full-time student and then I think two or three
of my summers I ended up going to summer school.
Right.
Do you remember what your major was those first
two semesters?
My first semester it was history.
Why?
Because I had always liked history.
I had thought of anthropology at some time
but it never really gels.
And then when political science department, I guess came,
sprung from history, I guess it was,
then I went into the political science.
But I started with history.
What did you think your career was going to be?
What did you hope you were going to?
Well, I had thought of being a lawyer at that time.
Why?
Well, I guess I began to get in my, to my courses.
At that time the Hispanic,
the Hispanic movement was beginning to get the notion
of young Hispanics becoming lawyers or doctors and, you know,
and promoting those professions became more and more.
And I, I guess I began to see myself as someone
who could become a lawyer and so that was, I mean,
I started thinking about that but I thought
about it more seriously obviously as I got.
Was there a person that was a role model
that you now realize somebody that, oh, he or she is a lawyer,
I'd like to be?
No, because quite frankly in Bryan there were no,
I didn't know really any lawyers.
I knew some of the judges here.
My brothers and I started going to a prayer meeting early,
early in the, when I was nine years old.
But I didn't, we didn't know the men that personally
but they were Judge Barron and some of the others
who were Judge Davis, W.C. Davis.
But no, I can't say that there was one person
who inspired me to say you can be a lawyer
or you ought to be a lawyer.
I just wanted to achieve.
I wanted to, to do the best I could and to be successful.
How did you dress in those days as an Aggie freshman?
Well, blue jeans and, and shirts, I mean,
t-shirts or, not t-shirts but it was another, it had shirts with,
I think it was blue jeans.
Because we had some bell-bottom pants
but they weren't that bell-bottom.
But mainly blue jeans, you know, and shirt.
Now were you clean-shaven then?
I had, well I, yes, I had a mustache.
And.
As a freshman?
No, my mustache came I think when I was
like a sophomore or junior.
I think that's when it began.
And.
Why I, I think my, I think my older brother started wearing
a mustache and I thought, well I'll try one.
But other than that, I mean, I, I didn't have a beard or anything.
And your hair was what length?
Oh, I would say it would, it would reach my shoulders.
It wasn't, it wasn't long.
I mean, it wasn't something that I had, I couldn't.
But it was, it was very, very manageable.
I mean, nowadays it would be considered relatively mild.
The first year or two, what did you and your,
your buddies do on a Saturday night to have fun?
Where did y'all go play?
Well, I guess we'd go to the movies downtown.
We'd play, we'd go bowling.
Go to pizza, get some pizza.
Of course, we'd go to football games.
There was always a, there was always those concerts before the
football games that we went to.
I can't say there was much, much more to do.
Did.
Was, was it normal for you and your buddies to go have beer somewhere?
No, because, well, because we, I didn't drink.
And, and the friends that we hung around with were basically non-drinkers also.
So that never really was one of those, one of those traditions or
activities we engaged in.
It was really kind of.
If you needed advice that first year, to whom did you turn other than a brother?
Did, was there anybody on campus that, with whom you turned?
No, no, no.
Was there anybody on campus with whom you, that you turned to for,
about what courses to take or?
No, not that first year.
Later there was a couple of people, but that.
Who, who'd it be then, later?
Was, Wayne Stark was probably one of the.
How did you meet him?
Well, my sophomore, latter second semester of my sophomore year, a friend of mine,
who was a graduate student, we started talking about.
Yeah.
establishing a group on campus, a Hispanic group, and Wayne Stark had worked with a lot
of the MSC groups, so we went to visit him and he was very, very supportive, very inspirational
about achievement.
He had worked with Henry Cisneros and Hector Gutierrez and a group
of other students that he had sort of mentored and worked with and that's why he was a guy
that could make you feel like, wow, I can accomplish something.
And so I was very appreciative
of Mr. Wayne Stark because he had this enthusiasm for making students feel good about themselves
and really asking good questions about why and what do you mean by that and I mean, I
mean.
Not to challenge one, but to make you think critically.
Now, you mentioned Hector and Henry, they were already gone, they had to graduate.
Yes, right, they were a few years ahead, but I was aware of them through, I can't remember
how, I guess just some different dialogue we had had and I met them later.
I mean, when
I was in college, I had met a couple of them.
Did y'all organize a group?
Yes, a group of us did and in fact it's called the Committee on the Awareness of the Mexican-American
Culture.
It's CAMOC, the Committee on the Awareness of the Mexican-American Culture.
One of the things that my buddies and I began to see and these were students that were in
the core, non-core, graduate students and undergrads.
One of the things that we noticed
that was that we felt that the reason why other students felt, you know, that they were
somewhat, they saw us differently was because they didn't understand the culture.
And so
we thought, why not let's form a group that talks about the Hispanic culture, the Mexican-American
culture, what it is, what it's not, and help in that vein.
And the MSC became the sponsor
group for that and so that's where I started getting involved with that activity.
All right, now let's cover the whole four years from here on.
Generalize about the Hispanic
student body here.
Where were they from, what was their background and so forth at A&M in
those years?
I think a lot of them came from San Antonio in the Valley.
There's a good number from
Houston.
Laredo had always a large number, the Laredo hometown club, there was always
a strong group.
And Yeah.
Yeah. Yeah.
Yeah. Yeah.
Yeah. Yeah.
Yeah.
And they were mainly boys because the girls were beginning to barely start coming in,
but it wasn't really a stronghold there.
I mean, I graduated in December of 73 so the
girls hadn't really started coming in.
The students came from families that were probably
middle income or better.
Were they first generation college students?
Or some of them second?
I would say they were.
I think it was the second generation.
A lot of them were second generation because their parents had gone to school somewhere else.
I heard later, which I thought made sense, from Laredo, a lot of the Aggie mothers there had gone.
There's kids that had gone to Laredo Community College.
And once they got out, they sent their kids.
And the mothers of these kids, they had gone to UT or had gone to some other school, but they sent their kids to A&M.
And I don't know what that connection was from the very beginning, but a lot of Mexican-American kids.
But there were also some of the kids who were very modest means.
I mean, one of my best friends, Steven Salinas, who's an engineer, finished with a chemical engineering degree with a 4.0.
And his brother finished here and went on to UT Medical Center.
Both came from a home in West Laco, one-room house.
And her mother was a nurse.
And they worked.
I mean, they were just poor.
Now, did you all create KAMAK because you wanted to keep people from harassing you?
Were you all being bothered?
No, no.
It wasn't harassment because it was never that physical.
That's what I thought.
But it was more of a sense of connectivity.
In other words, a lot of this was driven by what a lot of the other kids were feeling.
From Laredo and from the Valley.
Because for them, coming to this environment was really, really tough.
I was used to this environment.
So, I mean, to me, it was not such an environment that I was not used to.
I mean, being the only Hispanic in an all-white setting, that was not unusual.
But this was a very interesting time, I guess, the career, the life of this state.
In the late 60s, the Chicano, the so-called Hispanic Chicano movement was getting started.
So, you would begin to hear and see literature that talked about this.
Well, being 18, 19 years old, it was the kind of stuff that, okay, what is that all about?
So, it awakened a lot of ideas about culture, language, history.
Well, where is our history?
Where is our background?
Where do you see that?
So, it began to raise a lot of those questions.
So, it was one of those, the timing of it was just right.
Had it happened five years before, that wouldn't have been the case.
But it was a question of what was going on.
What was going on in a larger external part of this state that impacted us?
Were there some key professional staff and or faculty, other than Wayne Stark,
that were positive in helping you all?
Yeah.
I think Chuck McCandless was one of them.
He began to see the effort.
Billy Lay, the director of admissions at that time.
Mm-hmm.
Mm-hmm.
Mm-hmm.
Mm-hmm.
And we had worked with him because we were trying to recruit students.
Mm-hmm.
I mean, we wanted to see how could we help create a positive feeling for Hispanic students.
How could we help recruit students?
So, we kind of on our own took that as an opportunity.
We found ourselves to be somewhat privileged to be able to achieve what we were achieving going to college.
And we wanted to see how do we bring more students in.
I mean, it's some of the same thing that's happening now.
But I haven't heard you infer in any way that this was intended to be some kind of protest.
Oh, no.
No, no.
By no means.
Because, I mean, I think we saw the A&M family being big enough to welcome people who were very different.
And, by the way, at the same time, Hispanics and African Americans,
we sort of connected because there were very few African American students on campus.
And most of them were athletes.
So, there was a...
There was a connectivity there.
And they felt alienated.
They felt somewhat ostracized.
Not that anybody would intentionally do something and say, get out of here, whatever.
It was just there was not a social connection with a lot of the students.
All right.
In terms of vocabulary, in that era, normally, what did you and your associates,
what wording did you all use to identify yourselves?
As far as Latinos?
Yeah, yeah.
You weren't...
You weren't Chicano.
Well, no, there were Chicanos.
No.
Did you use that word very often?
Yes, a lot of the students did.
What was the preferable term?
I think the word Chicano, Mexican-American, it depended on what part of the state you were from.
No, I'm talking about here.
What I'm saying, when people came to school here from other parts of the state, some came
and they would say, don't ever call me Chicano because my parents, they don't like that word,
particularly when they're from the United States.
They don't like the people from the Valley.
Others, it was not such a bad...
In San Antonio, there was not such a bad deal.
It all depended on who it was and where they were from.
What was the most widely accepted term?
I said Chicano was because part of it was, again, there were groups that were getting
started that would use the word Chicano.
The word Chicano has always been part of our language, but it was used mainly among the
groups.
Here was, okay, let's surface.
This group is a word that describes us as a group versus what the government has decided
to call us, Mexican-American or whatever.
It was more or less, we've decided who we are and we're going to use this term because
it best describes us.
In makeup, almost every single person in your group was Mexican-American, not from any other
Latin background.
No, it was being Mexican-American.
Almost no Mexican nationals.
No, because most of those would have been probably more associated with the international
students.
Okay.
Let's see.
Did I cut off a while ago, you said Chuck McCandless.
Were there any other staff or faculty that were very helpful to you all during this time?
Billy Lay, like I said.
I think those were pretty much some of the...
The ones that struck out the most.
Did you have a sponsor?
Did you have to have an advisor?
Yes, and I don't remember who that was when it first started.
I don't remember who that was exactly.
Now, I'm assuming that in terms of religious affiliation, you would have been an exception
as a Methodist.
Yes, yes, because everybody else among the Hispanics were all Catholic.
That's what I would have...
But...
There were some good number of Baptist students who came.
Really?
Yes.
Okay.
So there was a...
In fact, my mother, at our home, every Sunday we would have...
Ever since my brothers were in college, and even when I went, we would invite friends
of ours to come eat lunch with us at my dad's house, at our house.
And my mother would make caldo de res, you know, broth of beef.
Mm-hmm.
And some mole, chicken mole.
And that was sort of our treat.
And we would have maybe anywhere from five to ten college students that would come over
and have...
I've had guys that they were five, six, eight years old, and they'd say,
Oh, I remember.
I know who you are.
I used to go to your house with your brothers all the time.
So, I mean, I've had that happen to me even a couple of years ago.
Let's see.
Back in student.
Life.
Did you and your buddies get involved as undergraduates in any kind of good bull,
or any pranks, any jokes?
Well, no.
It was really difficult to do, given the time that we had, limited time.
I wouldn't want to say that I was all serious and no play, but we were...
You have to understand, my parents had reared us pretty consistently.
And so, doing some pranks was pretty much not one of those things we did.
I really can't remember.
In terms of going to class, what were your rules about going to class,
and what kind of behavior did you try to follow in a class?
I tried to attend every class.
I was very...
As much as possible.
I guess my classes...
One of the things that I began to really enjoy a lot were my sociology and philosophy classes.
Doctor, he was a biologist, he taught a social biology course, older gentleman.
And he opened my eyes to a lot of discussions about the effects of ecology, the effects
of sociology and biology.
And so, those classes were...
Gary Halter was one of my professors.
They opened my eyes a lot to the whole notion of who I was, who we were as a society.
And again, politically speaking, things were changing in the state in 69 to 73.
I mean, there was a lot of Hispanic groups rising.
There was a lot of issues about race issues, ethnic issues across the country.
So, those were just really tough.
Yeah.
¿How did you get to know each other?
What was your family like?
Can you take it?
We were talking about key people.
And you said Gary Halter...
Doctor Van Riper, who at the time when I started...
And I remember him as the department head in political science.
He later on served as a reference for me to law school.
So obviously he was quite...
Did you have any courses with him?
Yes.
I took a graduate course with him in personnel management.
That's very interesting.
David? Yeah.
I'm a law student.
I did my MS.
I finished my master's in law.
I was a law student.
I was a law student.
I was, I took 12 hours in grad school, though my heart was not in it because of circumstances
that had changed for me.
I, when I finished college, I got admitted to St. Mary's Law School, but because I didn't
plan well enough, I was in love, my mind was a little confused, I decided to hold back.
And, but as good fortune happens, I was offered a job as assistant director of admissions
involving the recruitment of students.
So all those things being what they were, I thought, you know, I'll put off law school
for a while.
It all, it bothered me a lot, and of course later on I did, eight years later I did go
to law school, seven years later, but that was, that happened.
All right, all right.
And as you progressed?
As you progressed through A&M, what, was there someone that became your advisor that
you went to for advice and counsel?
There was a gentleman by the name of, my brain is just going out on me here, he was a political
science professor who happened to be a lawyer.
Jack Woods?
Jack Woods.
Describe him.
Jack Woods was a.
He was a professor, I guess he was an adjunct professor perhaps, but he was a lawyer.
And he was the first lawyer that I really got to know and talk, and he was, I took constitutional
law under him and judicial process under him.
And he really, really excited me about the law, and the class, the way he taught it.
And he was a person that I could talk to, and we talked a lot about, about school.
A couple of times I talked to him.
He was a great teacher.
Yeah.
He invited me over to go to his house.
In fact, he also served as a letter of reference for me.
And I guess it, what it did, his relationship with me and my relationship to him, helped
me with more ease and more comfort talk about, you know, what I wanted to do.
It wasn't so much just a strictly uncomfortable discussion, but a very comfortable discussion
about opportunities and challenges.
And he also happened to be a very strong faith-based individual who didn't mind sharing
that with me.
All right.
Now, was he or someone else the one who would help you pick out your courses your junior
and senior year?
No.
I don't remember if there was anybody in particular.
A lot of times it may have been the department secretary who knew about as much about what
was offered and what was not offered.
But, I mean, I think it was a combination of faculty like Gary Halter and Jack Woods
and Dr. Paul and Riper who, you know, you begin to, one began to kind of feel comfortable
with.
Looking back, were there any courses that you now realize were quite important to you?
I think my course in philosophy, my course in social biology, and my sociology courses
that I took.
I think they helped me think about a lot about communities and society.
And the courses I took with Jack Woods helped me think about what law school was going to
be like.
Was there any A&M-related experience that you now realize was life-changing to you?
All right.
Thank you.
Mm-hmm.
Oh, let me see here.
Life-changing.
No accidents?
No sheer coincidences?
No.
I don't think so.
No?
I mean, it was a...
I'm assuming you didn't flunk a course.
No, I didn't.
I didn't flunk a course.
I had a, I guess when I was a sophomore, I had a real eye-opener in my own personal life.
I had, and it really, for about six months to a year, I had a failed relationship with
this young lady.
And it really just shook me up in terms of what I wanted to do with my career, my work,
and my life.
And it really just shook me up because I had to really get ahold of my roots to say, what
am I going to do?
And as good fortune would have, I went to work in Chicago with the United Methodist
Church as a missionary, urban missionary.
And that really transformed my thinking about myself.
And when I came back to school my junior year...
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
My junior year, it really just, I was dedicated, and actually my grades started getting better
and made the dean's list about two or three times and, you know, really got myself back
on track.
How long were you up there?
First summer.
First summer.
What I would do in the summers is that I would work as a youth director in various churches.
I did that for three different, four different communities.
Now, one of my normal questions is about religious groups on the campus.
Your primary activity that was related to religion was at the United Methodist Church
in Bryan.
Is that right?
It was Oak Grove United Methodist Church.
It was a church my father founded.
Oak Grove.
Oh.
And where, what part of town is it?
It's in West Bryan, off of 21 and 6, and it's a Hispanic United Methodist Church.
How big was it then?
Oh, probably about 150 members.
Mm.
And so that's where you attended.
I attended there on Sundays and, but I also, during the week, the Baptist Student Union
had a great lunch on certain days and a great speaker.
And so my brother and a couple of my buddies, we would all, we'd come and eat at the BSU.
At the BSU.
At the BSU.
My son.
At the BSU.
And so that was my junior or senior year.
Mm-hmm.
And it was, it was great.
Now, did anybody ever tease you about a group of Methodists coming over to eat Baptist food
or anything like that?
No.
I think it was intended that everybody would, I think everybody, even from the Wesley Foundation,
went over there.
I mean, they were, the Wesley Foundation had their own, but it was not comparable.
It was just a great program they had there.
As far as you can recall, describe the religious groups and their activities on the campus
in those days.
Well, the most I can remember, of course, I remember going to the All-Face Chapel a
few times for a couple of, for some weddings, and I guess the strongest group that I remember
were the Baptists, because they had the Baptist Student Union and they would have ongoing
activities and sometimes you'd see stuff where they were inviting people to come.
But I don't recall any particular.
What about St. Mary's?
No.
And I, it was there, but I really didn't, it's right, it's right close to the Wesley
Foundation, but I never really was connected to that because just not being Catholic and,
strange enough, most of my friends were Protestants, they were Baptist or Methodist or other Protestants
had been in my interest.
And Religious Emphasis Week was long gone from A&M by then, wasn't it?
Yeah, I think so.
I mean, I don't remember that being celebrated.
That's what I thought.
It may have been, but I don't remember it.
Did you encounter many A&M students who were of some other religious background other than
Christianity?
A few Jews, but not very many.
How were they treated?
Same.
I mean, it was no, I don't think that the Jews, either in high school or in college,
I don't remember ever having any particular, I mean, I guess if we had a stereotype about
them is that they were, you know, they had a different way of, they didn't believe in
Jesus as a savior, but they were, I mean, for the most part, I think the Jews that I
encountered in high school here, the Polycoffs were two brothers that were.
